title: Der Herr Gevatter
source: Brüder Grimm, Kinder- und Hausmärchen, Band 1, 7. Auflage (Ausgabe letzter Hand), Dieterich, Göttingen 1857, KHM 42. Text from Wikisource (twice proofread against the original). 1857 spelling preserved exactly.
about: KHM 42 in the Grimms’ collection, a short and eerie tale. A poor man, out of godfathers, takes a stranger as godfather to his newest child; the stranger gives him a water that reveals whether the sick will live or die. Visiting the godfather’s house, he climbs past mounting horrors — a quarrelling shovel and broom, heaps of dead fingers and skulls, fish frying themselves — to find the godfather crowned with long horns, for the stranger is Death or the Devil, and the man flees. Spelling follows the 1857 edition, so you will meet older forms such as daß, Thor, Thüre, wußte, mußt, ward, todter and angethan.

sentences.t: Ein armer Mann hatte so viel Kinder, daß er schon alle Welt zu Gevatter gebeten hatte, und als er noch eins bekam, so war niemand mehr übrig, den er bitten konnte.
sentences.l: A poor man had so many children that he had already asked all the world to be godfather, and when he got yet another one, then there was no one left whom he could ask.
sentences.i: A poor man had so many children that he had already asked all the world to stand godfather, and when he had yet another, there was no one left whom he could ask.
sentences.n: “zu Gevatter bitten” = to ask someone to be godfather (to stand as godparent); the Gevatter is the child’s godfather and, by extension, a familiar term between the parent and that man. “daß” is the 1857 spelling of dass.

sentences.t: Er wußte nicht was er anfangen sollte, legte sich in seiner Betrübnis nieder und schlief ein.
sentences.l: He knew not what he should begin, laid himself down in his affliction, and fell asleep.
sentences.i: He did not know what to do, lay down in his distress, and fell asleep.
sentences.n: “wußte” is the 1857 spelling of wusste. “legte sich ... nieder” (sich niederlegen, to lie down) and “schlief ein” (einschlafen, to fall asleep) are both separable verbs — the prefixes nieder- and ein- jump to the end.

sentences.t: Da träumte ihm er sollte vor das Thor gehen und den ersten, der ihm begegnete, zu Gevatter bitten.
sentences.l: Then it dreamed to him that he should go before the gate and ask the first who met him to be godfather.
sentences.i: Then he dreamed that he should go out to the town gate and ask the first man he met to stand godfather.
sentences.n: “träumte ihm” is impersonal with a dative — literally ‘it dreamed to him,’ i.e. ‘he dreamed.’ “Thor” is the 1857 spelling of Tor (‘gate’), with the old -th-; here the town gate.

sentences.t: Als er aufgewacht war, beschloß er dem Traume zu folgen, gieng hinaus vor das Thor und den ersten, der ihm begegnete, bat er zu Gevatter.
sentences.l: When he had woken up, he resolved to follow the dream, went out before the gate, and the first who met him he asked to be godfather.
sentences.i: When he had woken, he resolved to follow the dream, went out to the gate, and asked the first man he met to stand godfather.
sentences.n: “beschloß” is the 1857 spelling of beschloss (beschließen, to resolve). “gieng” is the 1857 spelling of ging (‘went’). “dem Traume zu folgen” — folgen takes the dative (Traume has the old dative -e).

sentences.t: Der Fremde schenkte ihm ein Gläschen mit Wasser und sagte „das ist ein wunderbares Wasser, damit kannst du die Kranken gesund machen, du mußt nur sehen wo der Tod steht.
sentences.l: The stranger gave him a little glass of water and said, “that is a wondrous water; with it you can make the sick well — you must only see where Death is standing.
sentences.i: The stranger made him a present of a little glass of water and said, “This is a wondrous water; with it you can make the sick well — only you must see where Death is standing.
sentences.n: The stranger is no ordinary man: at the tale’s end his long horns give him away as the Devil (the water-and-Death motif also lets him be read as Death himself). “schenken” = to give as a gift. “die Kranken” is an adjective used as a noun, ‘the sick (people).’ “mußt” is the 1857 spelling of musst.

sentences.t: Steht er beim Kopf, so gib dem Kranken von dem Wasser, und er wird gesund werden, steht er aber bei den Füßen, so ist alle Mühe vergebens, er muß sterben.“
sentences.l: If he stands by the head, then give the sick one some of the water, and he will grow well; but if he stands by the feet, then all trouble is in vain — he must die.”
sentences.i: If he stands at the head, then give the sick person some of the water and he will get well; but if he stands at the feet, all your trouble is in vain — he must die.”
sentences.n: “Steht er ..., so ...” — a conditional formed by putting the verb first (‘if he stands ..., then ...’). “er” is Death (der Tod, masculine). Death at the head means the patient lives, at the feet that he dies — a widespread folk motif.

sentences.t: Der Mann konnte von nun an immer sagen ob ein Kranker zu retten war oder nicht, ward berühmt durch seine Kunst und verdiente viel Geld.
sentences.l: From now on the man could always say whether a sick person was to be saved or not, became famous through his art, and earned much money.
sentences.i: From then on the man could always tell whether a sick person could be saved or not; he grew famous through his art and earned a great deal of money.
sentences.n: “von nun an” = from now on. “ward” is the archaic preterite of werden (‘became’); modern German uses wurde, and you will meet ward throughout Grimm. “zu retten war” — the ‘sein + zu + infinitive’ construction, ‘was to be / could be saved.’

sentences.t: Einmal ward er zu dem Kind des Königs gerufen, und als er eintrat, sah er den Tod bei dem Kopfe stehen, und heilte es mit dem Wasser, und so war es auch bei dem zweitenmal, aber das drittemal stand der Tod bei den Füßen, da mußte das Kind sterben.
sentences.l: Once he was called to the king’s child, and when he stepped in, he saw Death standing by the head, and healed it with the water; and so it was also the second time; but the third time Death stood by the feet — then the child had to die.
sentences.i: Once he was called to the king’s child, and when he entered he saw Death standing at its head, and healed it with the water; and so it was the second time too; but the third time Death stood at the feet, and then the child had to die.
sentences.n: “eintrat” (eintreten, to step in, enter) is separable. “es” refers to das Kind (neuter), grammatically ‘it.’ “zweitenmal” / “drittemal” = the second time / the third time (das zweitemal, das drittemal).

sentences.t: Der Mann wollte doch einmal seinen Gevatter besuchen und ihm erzählen wie es mit dem Wasser gegangen war.
sentences.l: The man wanted for once to visit his godfather and tell him how it had gone with the water.
sentences.i: The man wanted, for once, to visit his godfather and tell him how things had gone with the water.
sentences.n: “doch einmal” = ‘for once, after all.’ “wie es ... gegangen war” — the impersonal es geht, ‘how it had gone / fared.’

sentences.t: Als er aber ins Haus kam, war eine so wunderliche Wirthschaft darin.
sentences.l: But when he came into the house, there was such strange goings-on within it.
sentences.i: But when he came into the house, there was such uncanny goings-on inside.
sentences.n: “wunderlich” = strange, odd, uncanny. “Wirthschaft” (1857 spelling of Wirtschaft) is literally ‘housekeeping,’ here in its extended sense of ‘state of affairs, goings-on.’ The old -th- also shows in Thor, Thüre.

sentences.t: Auf der ersten Treppe zankten sich Schippe und Besen, und schmissen gewaltig aufeinander los.
sentences.l: On the first stair a shovel and a broom were quarrelling, and flung mightily at each other.
sentences.i: On the first flight of stairs a shovel and a broom were quarrelling, laying into each other with a will.
sentences.n: “zankten sich” (sich zanken, to quarrel). “schmissen ... los” — losschmeißen, to let fly at; “aufeinander los” = at one another. This is the first of the mounting uncanny sights on the stairs, each with a sinister undertone the godfather will blandly explain away.

sentences.t: Er fragte sie „wo wohnt der Herr Gevatter?“
sentences.l: He asked them, “where does Mister Godfather live?”
sentences.n: “Herr Gevatter” — a courteous form of address, ‘Mister Godfather.’ “sie” (them) refers to the shovel and broom.

sentences.t: Der Besen antwortete „eine Treppe höher.“
sentences.l: The broom answered, “one stair higher.”
sentences.i: The broom answered, “one flight up.”

sentences.t: Als er auf die zweite Treppe kam, sah er eine Menge todter Finger liegen.
sentences.l: When he came onto the second stair, he saw a mass of dead fingers lying.
sentences.i: When he reached the second flight, he saw a heap of dead fingers lying there.
sentences.n: “todter” is the 1857 spelling of toter (‘dead,’ genitive plural, ‘of dead fingers’), keeping the old -d-t-. “eine Menge” = a lot, a mass of. The horrors escalate: a shovel and broom, now severed fingers.

sentences.t: Er fragte „wo wohnt der Herr Gevatter?“
sentences.l: He asked, “where does Mister Godfather live?”

sentences.t: Einer aus den Fingern antwortete „eine Treppe höher.“
sentences.l: One of the fingers answered, “one stair higher.”
sentences.i: One of the fingers answered, “one flight up.”

sentences.t: Auf der dritten Treppe lag ein Haufen todter Köpfe, die wiesen ihn wieder eine Treppe höher.
sentences.l: On the third stair lay a heap of dead heads, which directed him again one stair higher.
sentences.i: On the third flight lay a heap of dead men’s heads, and they directed him one flight higher again.
sentences.n: “wiesen” is the preterite plural of weisen (‘to point, direct’). “todter Köpfe” is genitive plural again (‘of dead heads’). The severed heads out-do the fingers — the escalation is deliberate.

sentences.t: Auf der vierten Treppe sah er Fische über dem Feuer stehen, die britzelten in der Pfanne, und backten sich selber.
sentences.l: On the fourth stair he saw fish standing over the fire, which sizzled in the pan and fried themselves.
sentences.i: On the fourth flight he saw fish standing over the fire; they sizzled in the pan and fried themselves.
sentences.n: “britzelten” (britzeln, a variant of brutzeln) = sizzled, sputtered. “backten” (backen) here in the sense ‘fried.’ “sich selber” = themselves — the fish cook themselves, the fourth uncanny sight.

sentences.t: Sie sprachen auch „eine Treppe höher.“
sentences.l: They too spoke, “one stair higher.”
sentences.i: They too said, “one flight up.”

sentences.t: Und als er die fünfte hinauf gestiegen war, so kam er vor eine Stube und guckte durch das Schlüsselloch, da sah er den Gevatter, der ein paar lange Hörner hatte.
sentences.l: And when he had climbed up the fifth, then he came before a room and peeped through the keyhole; there he saw the godfather, who had a pair of long horns.
sentences.i: And when he had climbed the fifth flight, he came to a room and peeped through the keyhole, and there he saw the godfather, who had a pair of long horns.
sentences.n: “hinauf gestiegen” (hinaufsteigen, to climb up) is separable; “die fünfte” = the fifth [stair]. The long horns give the godfather away: he is the Devil (or Death) himself. “Stube” = a room, parlour.

sentences.t: Als er die Thüre aufmachte und hinein gieng, legte sich der Gevatter geschwind aufs Bett und deckte sich zu.
sentences.l: When he opened the door and went in, the godfather quickly laid himself on the bed and covered himself up.
sentences.i: When he opened the door and went in, the godfather quickly lay down on the bed and covered himself up.
sentences.n: “Thüre” is the 1857 spelling of Tür (‘door’), with the old -th-. “machte ... auf” (aufmachen, to open), “gieng ... hinein” (hineingehen), and “deckte sich zu” (sich zudecken, to cover oneself) are all separable. The godfather hides his horns.

sentences.t: Da sprach der Mann „Herr Gevatter, was ist für eine wunderliche Wirthschaft in eurem Hause? als ich auf eure erste Treppe kam, so zankten sich Schippe und Besen mit einander und schlugen gewaltig auf einander los.“
sentences.l: Then the man spoke, “Mister Godfather, what a strange goings-on is there in your house? when I came onto your first stair, a shovel and a broom were quarrelling with one another and struck mightily at each other.”
sentences.i: Then the man said, “Mister Godfather, what uncanny goings-on there are in your house! When I came onto your first stair, a shovel and a broom were quarrelling with each other and laying into one another mightily.”
sentences.n: “was ist für eine ...” = ‘what a ... is that,’ an exclamatory question. “eurem / eure” — the man addresses the godfather with the old plural-of-respect ihr (‘you’). “mit einander” and “auf einander” are written as two words here (modern miteinander, aufeinander).

sentences.t: „Wie seid ihr so einfältig,“ sagte der Gevatter, „das war der Knecht und die Magd, die sprachen mit einander.“
sentences.l: “How simple you are,” said the godfather, “that was the farmhand and the maid, who were talking with one another.”
sentences.i: “How simple-minded you are,” said the godfather; “that was the farmhand and the maidservant, talking together.”
sentences.n: “seid ihr” — the respectful ihr again (‘you are’). “einfältig” = simple, naive. The godfather’s bland ‘explanation’ only deepens the menace: he turns severed fingers and quarrelling tools into servants and vegetables.

sentences.t: „Aber auf der zweiten Treppe sah ich todte Finger liegen.“
sentences.l: “But on the second stair I saw dead fingers lying.”
sentences.n: “todte” is the 1857 spelling of tote (‘dead,’ here accusative plural).

sentences.t: „Ei, wie seid ihr albern! das waren Skorzenerwurzel.“
sentences.l: “Ei, how foolish you are! those were salsify roots.”
sentences.i: “Why, how silly you are! those were salsify roots.”
sentences.n: “Ei” is an exclamation of mild reproach (‘why!, come now!’). “albern” = silly, foolish. “Skorzenerwurzel” = salsify / black salsify (Schwarzwurzel), a long, pale, finger-like root — the godfather’s grimly plausible cover for the dead fingers.

sentences.t: „Auf der dritten Treppe lag ein Haufen Todtenköpfe.“
sentences.l: “On the third stair lay a heap of death’s-heads.”
sentences.i: “On the third flight lay a heap of skulls.”
sentences.n: “Todtenköpfe” is the 1857 spelling of Totenköpfe (‘death’s-heads, skulls’).

sentences.t: „Dummer Mann, das waren Krautköpfe.“
sentences.l: “Stupid man, those were cabbage-heads.”
sentences.i: “Foolish man, those were heads of cabbage.”
sentences.n: A grim pun: Todtenköpfe (skulls, ‘dead-heads’) become Krautköpfe (cabbage-heads) — German calls a head of cabbage a Krautkopf, literally a ‘head.’

sentences.t: „Auf der vierten sah ich Fische in der Pfanne, die britzelten, und backten sich selber.“
sentences.l: “On the fourth I saw fish in the pan, which sizzled and fried themselves.”
sentences.i: “On the fourth I saw fish in the pan; they sizzled and fried themselves.”

sentences.t: Wie er das gesagt hatte, kamen die Fische und trugen sich selber auf.
sentences.l: As he had said that, the fish came and served themselves up.
sentences.i: As soon as he had said that, the fish came and served themselves up.
sentences.n: “trugen sich ... auf” (auftragen, to serve up, bring to table) is separable — the fish carry themselves to the table. Unlike the other sights, this one the godfather does not explain away; it simply happens.

sentences.t: „Und als ich die fünfte Treppe heraufgekommen war, guckte ich durch das Schlüsselloch einer Thür, und da sah ich Euch, Gevatter, und ihr hattet lange lange Hörner.“
sentences.l: “And when I had come up the fifth stair, I peeped through the keyhole of a door, and there I saw you, godfather, and you had long, long horns.”
sentences.n: “heraufgekommen” (heraufkommen, to come up) is separable. “Euch / ihr / hattet” — the respectful ihr (‘you’), with hattet ‘you had.’ The doubled “lange lange” intensifies (‘long, long’). The horns mark him as the Devil.

sentences.t: „Ei, das ist nicht wahr.“
sentences.l: “Ei, that is not true.”
sentences.i: “Why, that is not true.”

sentences.t: Dem Mann ward angst, und er lief fort, und wer weiß was ihm der Herr Gevatter sonst angethan hätte.
sentences.l: To the man it grew fearful, and he ran away, and who knows what else Mister Godfather would have done to him.
sentences.i: The man grew afraid and ran away — and who knows what else Mister Godfather would have done to him.
sentences.n: “Dem Mann ward angst” — an impersonal dative idiom, ‘the man became afraid’ (literally ‘to the man it became anxious’); ward is the archaic preterite of werden. “lief fort” (fortlaufen, to run away) is separable. “angethan” is the 1857 spelling of angetan (antun, ‘to do to someone’); “hätte angethan” is subjunctive II, ‘would have done.’
